topic: Topic 5: Social Activities, Fitness and Health
subject: spanish

items.q: What is the Spanish word for 'mobile phone' (in Spain)?
items.misconception: El celular (which is used in Latin America).
items.why: Vocabulary varies by region. Spain: móvil, ordenador (computer), coche (car). Latin America: celular, computadora, carro/auto. Students mix these. Examiners accept both, but consistency matters. Móvil is most common in GCSE/A-level.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Spain' in Spanish?
items.why: Students drop the 'a' or use a conjugated verb. The near future is IR (conjugated) + A + INFINITIVE. The 'a' is obligatory: voy A comer, vamos A ver, vas A estudiar. Heavily tested for holiday/future plans.

items.q: In Spain, how do you address a group of friends informally?
items.why: Students don't distinguish vosotros (informal plural, Spain) from ustedes (formal plural in Spain, but used for all plural in Latin America). In Spain: tú/vosotros = informal; usted/ustedes = formal. Latin America: only ustedes for plural.
It's Me duelen los pies.. “Me duele los pies.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which sentence advises 'You should stop smoking'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Deberías dejar de fumar.. “Debes dejar de fumando.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many hours of sleep per night are typically recommended for teenagers?
items.why: The real figure is 9 hours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
